role_summary: Co-founder and first president of the Southern Christian Leadership Conference (1957-1968); Nobel Peace Prize laureate (1964); the spokesperson the cultural reception of the civil rights movement most centers on.

photo.alt: Dr. Martin Luther King Jr., White House meeting with President Lyndon B. Johnson, March 18, 1966
photo.photographer: Yoichi Okamoto (1915-1985), Official White House Photographer
photo.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via Lyndon Baines Johnson Library and Museum)

ai_reading: King is discussed in seven transcripts: Judy Richardson (entry 69, Eyes on the Prize documentary producer), Joseph Echols Lowery (entry 66, SCLC co-founder), William S. Leventhal (entry 129, the athlete-activist cluster), Wyatt Tee Walker (entry 132, King's chief of staff), Martha Prescod Norman Noonan (entry 83, SNCC), C. T. Vivian (entry 134, SCLC), and Betty Garman Robinson (entry 7, SNCC). The cluster mixes SCLC inner circle (Lowery, Walker, Vivian), the documentation tradition (Richardson), and SNCC voices, the embedding pulling King's name across the corpus's institutional boundaries. The Leventhal cosine (with its athlete-activist cluster) is the surprise: King and the 1968 Cleveland Summit are connected in the cultural record but not as a clean cluster.
biographical_paragraph: Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. is discussed in this corpus by 7 interviewees across the catalog (Judy Richardson (entry 69), Joseph Echols Lowery (entry 66), William S. Leventhal (entry 129), Wyatt Tee Walker (entry 132), Martha Prescod Norman Noonan (entry 83), C. T. Vivian (entry 134), Betty Garman Robinson (entry 7)), but was not interviewed for the Civil Rights History Project; this catalog page treats Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. as an external figure whose presence in the archive is mediated through other voices. The canonical scholarly source the catalog cites for biographical orientation appears as [src: 1] below.

interview_snippets.speaker: Samuel Berry McKinney
interview_snippets.lead_in: Samuel Berry McKinney, who entered Morehouse College in 1944, recalls King as a fellow first-year student admitted early under a wartime acceleration program:
interview_snippets.quote: One of the classmates, one of the fellow freshmen, was a fellow from Atlanta, a little fellow, because of him. Squirt, named Martin Luther King Jr. was in the same freshman class.

interview_snippets.speaker: William G. Anderson
interview_snippets.lead_in: William G. Anderson, who became the first president of the Albany Movement, dates his acquaintance with King to before King held a doctorate, through his wife's family in Atlanta:
interview_snippets.quote: So I moved up to Atlanta with my new wife, who I met Martin Luther King, Jr. And that was prophetic. I did not plan that. He was not Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. then. He was just Martin Luther King Jr.

interview_snippets.speaker: Dorothy Foreman Cotton
interview_snippets.lead_in: Dorothy Foreman Cotton, later director of the SCLC Citizenship Education Program, describes the Petersburg, Virginia mass meeting that first brought her into King's orbit:
interview_snippets.quote: And I had heard something of non-violence and we invited this young preacher from Montgomery, Alabama, Martin Luther King to come up to Petersburg, Virginia.

interview_snippets.speaker: Reverend Harry Blake
interview_snippets.lead_in: Reverend Harry Blake, who became an SCLC field secretary covering Louisiana, recounts how King hired him onto the staff after a 1960 interview in Atlanta:
interview_snippets.quote: And in February of 1960, Martin invited me, Dr. King invited me to Atlanta to interview me and hired me on his staff. That was my first connection with him.

interview_snippets.speaker: William Lucy
interview_snippets.lead_in: William Lucy, the AFSCME official central to the 1968 Memphis sanitation strike, describes how King framed the strikers' condition:
interview_snippets.quote: What Dr. Martin Luther King Jr did was make this analogy that here is the problem in America. You've got people who work every day but don't earn a sufficient enough wage to get themselves out of poverty.

sources.title: David J. Garrow, Bearing the Cross: Martin Luther King, Jr., and the Southern Christian Leadership Conference
